_meta.method: ChEMBL bioactivity sweep filtered to fungal-source compounds. Two-pass approach: (1) bulk bioactivity pulls per chokepoint target (top 200 records by IC50) to inventory named compounds, (2) targeted compound→all-targets queries for known fungal natural products to surface chokepoint hits not captured in the top-200 cut. Confidence assignments use ChEMBL's `natural_product` flag + literature-confirmed biosynthetic-source organism.

targets_no_chembl_record: TXNIP — no curated single-protein ChEMBL target
headline_findings: FUMITREMORGIN C (Aspergillus fumigatus) → ABCG2 IC50 = 800 nM (pChEMBL 6.10) — sub-μM, canonical ABCG2 reference inhibitor in the literature
MYCOPHENOLIC ACID (Penicillium brevicompactum) → PPARγ partial agonist, low-potency (Kd ≈ 110 μM, flagged 'Outside typical range')
LOVASTATIN (Aspergillus terreus / Pleurotus ostreatus / Monascus ruber / red yeast rice) → HDAC6 IC50 = 16.3 μM (pChEMBL 4.79); also weak PPARγ Potency 50 μM
C5aR1 returned ZERO fungal-source hits (corroborates validation-experiments §1.21 — no validated natural-product C5aR1 antagonists in ChEMBL)

URAT1, GLUT9, NLRP3, IL-1β, TNF, Caspase-1, ASC, OAT1/3/4, KEAP1, Lp-PLA2, PDI returned no fungal-source compound hits in top-200 IC50 records OR direct compound→target queries for the canonical fungal natural products tested

URAT1.notes: Top hits are all synthetic uricosurics or plant flavonoids (morin from Maclura pomifera). No ChEMBL records of fungal-source compounds against URAT1 in the IC50 top-200 OR in direct compound→target probes for lovastatin / mycophenolic acid / cordycepin / griseofulvin / fumitremorgin / mevastatin.

GLUT9.notes: ChEMBL bioactivity coverage for GLUT9 is sparse (recently characterized urate transporter; structure solved 2024). No fungal compounds queried surfaced here. Most GLUT9 modulators in the literature are flavonoid/synthetic chemotype.

potency.assay_description: Inhibition of ABCG2 expressed in human NCI-H460 cells assessed as inhibition of PhA accumulation
potency.additional_data: Multiple records 11000 nM (>) to 800 nM (=); used as canonical ABCG2 reference inhibitor in mitoxantrone efflux assays (Rabindran et al. and many follow-ups, e.g. Bioorg Med Chem 2008, 2010, 2011; J Med Chem 2005, 2009)

fungal_compounds.confidence: high — ChEMBL `natural_product:true`; well-documented Aspergillus fumigatus indolyl diketopiperazine; FTC is the historical pharmacological tool compound for ABCG2 / BCRP discrimination from MDR1; multiple independent labs across 2005–2011

potency.note: 15 records total, none against ABCG2 in ChEMBL. Activity profile is antitrypanosomal + antifungal MIC. Listed here for completeness as a fumitremorgin congener.

fungal_compounds.confidence: medium — confirmed fungal NP, but chokepoint relevance not in ChEMBL; could be tested but no literature precedent

ABCG2.notes: FUMITREMORGIN C is the standout fungal-source ABCG2 hit and the highest-leverage finding from this entire sweep. The ABCG2 top-200 also contains many flavonoids/methylated-flavonoids (chrysin, retusin, ayanin, kaempferol, apigenin, genistein, quercetin, nobiletin, sinensetin, tangeretin, etc.) — predominantly plant-source but some have fungal endophyte production routes. Strict fungal-source only counts FTC.

Xanthine_Oxidase.notes: All top hits are synthetic (febuxostat/allopurinol/bropirimine) or plant-derived flavonoids. No fungal compounds surfaced in IC50 top-200, and direct probes of lovastatin/cordycepin/mycophenolic acid/fumitremorgin against XO returned zero ChEMBL records. Some Ganoderma triterpenoids have been reported as XO inhibitors in the Chinese-language literature but are not in ChEMBL.

NLRP3.notes: Very thin ChEMBL coverage for NLRP3 (only 8 named compounds with potency data) — most NLRP3 inhibitor work is in non-ChEMBL-curated mechanism literature (MCC950/CRID3, OLT1177, oridonin). No fungal-source NLRP3 hits in ChEMBL.

ASC_PYCARD.notes: ASC has minimal ChEMBL coverage (recent target ID, structural characterization 2014+). No fungal hits.

Caspase-1.notes: ChEMBL coverage for caspase-1 is dominated by synthetic peptidomimetic VX-765 / pralnacasan / Z-VAD-FMK chemotypes. No fungal-source small molecules surfaced.

IL-1beta.notes: Direct IL-1β small-molecule modulator chemistry is sparse; canakinumab/anakinra/rilonacept are biologics and not in ChEMBL bioactivity. No fungal hits.

TNFalpha.notes: No fungal hits. TNF small-molecule space is dominated by SPD-304-class direct inhibitors and synthetic IKK / NF-κB pathway compounds; no fungal-NP records surfaced.

C5aR1.notes: STRATEGIC NEGATIVE: ChEMBL has only 2 named compounds against C5aR1 (one peptide, one synthetic small molecule), and neither is fungal-derived. This corroborates validation-experiments.md §1.21 conclusion that there are no validated natural-product C5aR1 antagonists. C5aR1 looks like a pharmacochemistry-only target — fungal compounds are unlikely to provide a hit. Implication: do NOT spend wet-lab time screening mushroom extracts against C5aR1.

Lp-PLA2.notes: Lp-PLA2 ChEMBL coverage is dominated by darapladib-class synthetic chemistry. No fungal hits.

fungal_compounds.source_organism: Aspergillus terreus / Pleurotus ostreatus / Monascus ruber

potency.assay_description: Inhibition of recombinant HDAC6 (unknown origin) after 10 mins by fluorimetric analysis
potency.reference: J Med Chem 2013 (replicated in 2015 and 2016 follow-ups)

fungal_compounds.confidence: high (fungal source) but LOW POTENCY — pChEMBL 4.79 is mid-μM, well below drug-like cutoff. Lovastatin is overwhelmingly used as an HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor; the HDAC6 activity is a documented off-target rather than a primary mechanism. Mevastatin (CHEMBL54440, Penicillium citrinum) was tested in the same assay with no inhibition (-19% / -7% — i.e., inactive).

HDAC6.notes: Trichostatin A and Romidepsin (FK228) are KNOWN HDAC modulators but are bacterial (Streptomyces hygroscopicus / Chromobacterium violaceum) — NOT fungal. Excluded per scope.

potency.kd_flag: Outside typical range — Bioorg Med Chem Lett 2007 binding assay; treated as low-confidence
potency.additional_data: Antagonist activity at pioglitazone-stimulated PPARγ transcriptional activity in U2OS cells at 1 μM (Active); also activates PPARγ transcriptional activity in U2OS at 0.01–1 μM (Active); 74% inhibition of [14C]acetate uptake in 3T3-L1 cells at 2 μM

fungal_compounds.confidence: medium — well-confirmed fungal NP, real PPARγ functional activity in cells, but Kd is outside typical range and the dose-response pattern (antagonist of pioglitazone-stimulated activity AND independent activator) suggests partial/biased agonism. Probably more useful as mechanism probe than therapeutic.

potency.assay_description: PUBCHEM_BIOASSAY: qHTS assay for small molecule antagonists of PPARγ signaling

fungal_compounds.confidence: low — PubChem qHTS hit at 50 μM with 'inconclusive' annotation; not a meaningful PPARγ ligand

PPARgamma.notes: PPARγ is a promiscuous orphan-class lipid receptor that binds many fatty acids and natural products at low affinity. The fungal hits here are real but weak (Kd > 100 μM). Could be a chassis for synergy mapping but unlikely to be a primary lead.

KEAP1.notes: KEAP1 ChEMBL chemistry is dominated by synthetic Nrf2-pathway activators and KEAP1-Nrf2 PPI inhibitors. Sulforaphane / curcumin / dimethyl fumarate are plant/synthetic, not fungal. No fungal hits.

OAT1.notes: OAT1/3/4 chemistry in ChEMBL is dominated by uricosurics and probenecid-class synthetic compounds. No fungal hits.

OAT4.notes: Same as OAT1; minimal ChEMBL coverage overall.

TXNIP.notes: No curated single-protein ChEMBL target found for TXNIP. TXNIP modulator chemistry is small and predominantly synthetic (e.g. SRI-37330). Skipped.

PDI_P4HB.notes: PDI ChEMBL chemistry is dominated by synthetic disulfide-targeted electrophiles (e.g. CCF642, RB-11-ca). No fungal hits.

summary_table_compound_target.tier: TIER 1 — drug-like potency, canonical reference inhibitor

summary_table_compound_target.best_potency: functional activity at 1 μM (cell-based, Active); Kd ≈ 110 μM (out-of-range)
summary_table_compound_target.tier: TIER 3 — real but weak; useful as mechanism probe

limitations: ChEMBL source-organism field is sparsely populated: many fungal compounds will be missed because the bioactivity record doesn't cross-reference back to the compound's biosynthetic source. We mitigated this by using the compound-level `natural_product:true` flag plus literature-known fungal source organisms, but the ground truth is whatever ChEMBL's curators have entered.

Western-pharma curation bias: ChEMBL inherits PubMed/patent-indexed Western chemistry. TCM-source mushroom compounds (Ganoderma triterpenoids, Inonotus polysaccharide-bound xanthines, Cordyceps adenosines beyond cordycepin, Hericium erinaceus terpenoids, polyporus polysaccharopeptides) are underrepresented or absent. Phase 2b (LOTUS) and 2c (PubMed/CNKI/J-STAGE multilingual lit scan) should fill this gap.

ChEMBL bioactivity coverage is heavily biased toward enzyme-class and GPCR-class targets; transporters (URAT1, GLUT9, OAT1/3/4, ABCG2 except for the well-curated FTC reference) and protein-protein interactors (NLRP3 / ASC / Caspase-1 / KEAP1 / TXNIP) have shallow ChEMBL records. Lack of fungal hits against these targets is partly a coverage artifact, not necessarily a true biological null.

We tested only the canonical fungal natural products available in ChEMBL by name (lovastatin, mevastatin, mycophenolic acid, griseofulvin, cordycepin, fumitremorgin C, cyclotryprostatin A, polyporenic acid C). Mushroom-specific compound classes that ChEMBL does NOT have as named molecules — ganoderic acids A/B/C/D, ganoderiol, lucidenic acids, ergosterol, ergosta-7,22-dien-3β-ol, hericenones C–H, erinacines A/B/C, inotodiol, betulinic acid (CHEMBL269277, also has plant sources), pachymic acid, eburicoic acid, polysaccharide-K (PSK), eritadenine, terreusinone, aspulvinones, orsellinic acid — were searched and confirmed absent from ChEMBL. These need to be picked up by Phase 2b LOTUS pull.

The ABCG2 IC50 of 800 nM for fumitremorgin C is from a single primary source (J Nat Prod 2011) using a PhA accumulation assay; other ABCG2 assays in ChEMBL report higher values (>11 μM) or non-IC50 metrics. The 800 nM number is the most cited literature value but should be checked against the original Rabindran/Allen primary characterization (J Biol Chem 2000 / Cancer Res 2000) which is the source typically credited for FTC's ABCG2 specificity.

ABCG2 sweep contains many flavonoid / methylated-flavonoid hits (chrysin, kaempferol, apigenin, genistein, quercetin, retusin, ayanin, nobiletin, sinensetin, tangeretin, boeravinones, calycopterones, pinostrobin) — these are predominantly plant-derived but some have fungal endophyte production routes. We did not attempt to disambiguate per-compound; if the project later wants 'plant + fungal endophyte' rather than strict 'fungal only,' this list expands ~10×. Same caveat applies to the XO sweep flavonoid set.
